result:
The sun, a hazy orange orb this evening, is painting long shadows across my garden. My ancient lavender bushes, usually vibrant purple, are now muted tones of grey and sage. I can hear the gentle hum of bees, still hard at work, desperately trying to squeeze in the last few drops of nectar before nightfall. It’s a peaceful sound, a lullaby to usher in the twilight hours.

I’ve been thinking a lot lately about impermanence. How everything, eventually, fades. The lavender will wither in the winter, the bees will retreat to their hive, and even the sun, that life-giving force, will eventually burn out. It's a slightly melancholic thought, but also oddly comforting. Knowing that nothing stays the same allows me to appreciate the beauty of the present moment.

This fleeting scene in my garden, the warmth on my skin from the late afternoon sun, the sweet scent of lavender – these things are precious because they're temporary. They won’t last forever, and that’s okay. Instead of clinging to them, I can simply observe and enjoy them while they exist, letting the knowledge of their eventual disappearance amplify their beauty.

The garden, in its quiet, unassuming way, is a constant reminder of this. Growth, death, decay, renewal – it’s all part of the cycle, and it’s all beautiful.
